title: Hormuz Erupts Again as US Revokes Iran Oil Waiver and Resumes Strikes
subhead: The US-Iran ceasefire is in tatters after a weekend of mutual strikes over the Strait of Hormuz, with Washington revoking Iran's oil sanctions waiver and oil prices surging nearly 5%. Meanwhile, Russia banned diesel exports after Ukrainian drone strikes crippled refineries, and the UK seized a Russian shadow fleet tanker in the English Channel.

sections.heading: Strait of Hormuz: Ceasefire Unravels as US and Iran Trade Strikes
sections.body:
The US-Iran interim deal, already fragile, appears to be collapsing into open conflict over the Strait of Hormuz. The US military launched a third round of strikes against Iran on July 11-12 after Iranian forces attacked a commercial cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, leaving a civilian crew member missing, according to CENTCOM. Iran's IRGC declared the strait closed, while the US insisted ships were still transiting. More than 10 projectiles struck Iran's Gulf island of Qeshm on Sunday, Iranian state media reported. Iran also expanded attacks onto US-allied Gulf states, marking a dangerous escalation beyond the bilateral US-Iran confrontation.

Oil prices surged nearly 5% on July 13, with Brent crude advancing $3.34 to $79.50/barrel and WTI gaining $3.07, as markets priced in the renewed disruption to the world's most critical energy chokepoint. The fighting is the latest chapter in a conflict that has see-sawed between deal and open warfare since the June 14 memorandum of understanding, which Trump hailed with 'Ships of the World, start your engines.' A New York Times analysis on July 12 argued that the deal's language effectively formalized Iranian control of the strait, a contention that now appears borne out by events on the water.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz ceasefire was supposed to end the oil shock. Its collapse means the 20% of global oil that transits the strait is back at risk, and oil markets are repricing upward. The conflict's spread to Gulf allies of the US raises the stakes far beyond a bilateral dispute.

sections.heading: US Revokes Iran Oil Waiver, Ending Brief Resumption of Iranian Crude Sales
sections.body:
The Trump administration revoked the sanctions waiver that had temporarily allowed Iranian oil and petrochemical sales, following Iran's attacks on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. The waiver, granted as part of the June 14 memorandum of understanding, had briefly permitted Tehran to sell oil — a provision Trump once celebrated as part of reopening the strait. Reuters and multiple outlets confirmed the revocation on July 8.

The waiver's cancellation hits a market already dealing with supply confusion. Iranian oil floating storage at sea had surged as Tehran ramped up exports during the brief peace window, but China's teapot refiners turned to cheaper crude from Iraq, the UAE, and Qatar instead, according to Reuters. Now those Iranian cargoes are stranded, with no legal buyer and a revoked American blessing. India, which had begun purchasing Iranian crude under the waiver, faces supply disruption, according to Mathrubhumi English.
sections.whyItMatters: The revocation eliminates any near-term path for Iranian oil to legally reach the market, tightening global supply at exactly the moment Hormuz is again under fire. China's teapot refiners had already diversified away, signaling that even when legal, Iranian crude faces demand headwinds.

sections.heading: Russia Bans Diesel Exports After Ukrainian Drone Strikes Cripple Refineries
sections.body:
Russia imposed a full ban on diesel exports starting July 8, after a sustained Ukrainian drone campaign devastated its refining capacity. Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak announced the ban at a government meeting chaired by Putin, adding that Russia would also begin importing petroleum products from Belarus and Kazakhstan to stabilize domestic supply. The move followed major Ukrainian strikes: on July 8, Ukrainian drones hit three Russian oil refineries, tankers on the Sea of Azov, and pipeline pumping stations in what Reuters called 'a major night of strikes' ranging from the Ukrainian border to the Urals. On July 10, the Ilsky refinery in Krasnodar caught fire after a drone attack. On July 12, Ukrainian drones struck the Syzran refinery in Samara Oblast.

The export ban has roiled global diesel markets, with European diesel prices spiking sharply, according to EADaily and The Star. Russia's domestic fuel crisis is severe: the Kremlin is subsidizing pump prices 30-50% below market rates at a cost of at least 200 billion rubles ($3 billion) per month, according to United24 Media. Kazakhstan has restricted Russian vehicle entries to once per day to combat fuel smuggling. Russia also lowered environmental fuel standards from Euro-5 to Euro-3.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia was the world's largest diesel exporter. Its exit from the market tightens an already strained global diesel supply — and proves that Ukraine's drone campaign has achieved what sanctions alone could not: a meaningful reduction in Russian fuel export capacity.

sections.heading: UK Seizes Russian Shadow Fleet Tanker in First-of-Its-Kind Operation
sections.body:
British Royal Marine Commandos boarded and seized the MV Smyrtos, a Russian shadow fleet oil tanker, in a six-hour operation in the English Channel on June 15. The operation, the first time UK armed forces have intercepted a sanctioned vessel in this way, saw Marines fast-rope from helicopters onto the Cameroon-flagged ship. The National Crime Agency arrested an Indian national, 38-year-old Ajay Pant, on suspicion of sanctions offences. He faces up to 10 years in prison under UK sanctions laws. The 24 remaining Georgian and Indian crew are assisting investigations.

PM Keir Starmer called it 'another blow to Russia' and a warning to 'those fuelling Putin's war.' The MoD said the shadow fleet of 700+ vessels carries 75% of Russia's sanctioned oil. The ship had sailed from Russia's Ust-Luga port on June 5. Other tankers in the area turned around following the seizure, according to BBC reporting. Moscow described the operation as 'bordering on international piracy.' This came alongside a new Canadian sanctions package targeting 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy revenues, announced by PM Carney at the G7 summit.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first direct seizure of a shadow fleet vessel by a Western military force, not just a port denial. If other nations replicate the approach, it could disrupt Russia's primary sanctions-evasion mechanism. The deterrent effect was immediate: other shadow fleet tankers turned around in the Channel.

sections.heading: France-UK Hormuz Naval Mission Stalls as Fighting Resumes
sections.body:
France and Britain's multinational naval mission to safeguard Strait of Hormuz shipping — backed by about 20 countries and announced at the June G7 summit — now faces an uncertain future as the ceasefire it was designed to support has collapsed. France committed its nuclear-powered aircraft carrier Charles de Gaulle, two minehunters, and said the force could deploy 'within two or three days.' Italy offered two minehunters; Germany pledged a Mediterranean-based minehunter. Britain sent a destroyer. Demining operations were estimated to take 40-50 days before normal traffic could resume, according to the Saudi Gazette.

However, the mission's viability depends on Iranian consent, which appears nonexistent. Indonesia publicly declined to participate. Iran has not accepted any Western naval presence, and the renewed fighting makes escort operations impractical. According to France 24, shipping and seafarers' associations said leaders had not given enough detail for ships to resume transits, with BIMCO's chief security officer stating: 'We still consider it very risky for ships to commence transits at this point.' At least five LNG carriers resumed Hormuz transits in early July, according to Anadolu Agency, but the latest escalation likely reverses that momentum.
sections.whyItMatters: The coalition mission was the West's main answer to restoring Hormuz shipping. Its deployment now depends on a ceasefire that doesn't exist. The 40-50 day demining timeline means even a durable peace wouldn't quickly restore normal traffic.

sections.heading: Ukraine Creates Long-Range Strike Command as Drone War on Russian Energy Escalates
sections.body:
Ukraine has created a dedicated 'long-range' command to coordinate deep strikes on Russian territory, according to Reuters on July 10. The move formalizes a drone campaign that has progressively degraded Russia's refining capacity. Over the past week, Ukrainian drones struck three refineries in a single night (July 8), hit the Ilsky refinery in Krasnodar (July 10), damaged tankers on the Sea of Azov, and struck the Syzran refinery in Samara Oblast (July 12). Ukraine also reported hitting 21 vessels — Russian fuel tankers supplying Crimea — and Russian pipeline pumping stations.

The cumulative impact is significant: Russia's refining capacity has been reduced enough that Moscow was forced to ban diesel exports and begin importing fuel. The campaign has effectively turned Ukraine's drone force into an energy weapon that sanctions never fully achieved. Russia's Black Sea Fleet command is also reportedly preparing to relocate from occupied Crimea to Novorossiysk, according to Ukrainian partisan sources cited by the Kyiv Independent.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine has systematized its energy-strike capability with a permanent command structure. The result — Russia exiting the global diesel market — shows that drone warfare can achieve strategic energy disruption that economic sanctions alone could not.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals: India-Indonesia Rare Earth Partnership and Turkey's NATO-Emerging Role
sections.body:
India and Indonesia signed agreements on rare earth supply chain cooperation and BrahMos missile sales during PM Modi's visit to Jakarta on July 7, according to Firstpost. The partnership aims to diversify critical mineral supply chains away from Chinese dominance, with Indonesia's tin and rare earth reserves offering an alternative feedstock for India's defense and electronics industries.

Separately, a Daily Sabah op-ed argued that Turkey's Beylikova rare earth reserve should be on NATO's strategic agenda, positioning Turkey as a potential non-Chinese rare earth supplier for the alliance. Meanwhile, US critical minerals stocks rallied on July 7 after the Department of Energy selected Peabody for funding to advance domestic rare earth and critical mineral production, and Critical Metals Corp's REalloys subsidiary secured a 15-year binding offtake agreement with the US Army, according to Invezz. And at a Rio conference, Lula adviser Celso Amorim framed rare earths and data sovereignty as strategic priorities for the Global South through BRICS, as reported by SCMP.
sections.whyItMatters: The scramble for non-Chinese rare earth supply chains is accelerating across multiple continents simultaneously. India-Indonesia, US Army offtakes, and Turkey's NATO pitch all point to the same trend: critical mineral diversification is moving from policy papers to signed contracts and military procurement.

bottomLine: The weekend's events shattered any remaining illusion that the US-Iran ceasefire was holding. With Washington revoking Iran's oil waiver and launching its third round of strikes in a week, Iran attacking commercial shipping and declaring Hormuz closed, and oil surging toward $80, the energy market's brief reprieve is over. Meanwhile, Ukraine's systematic drone campaign has accomplished what Western sanctions couldn't: forcing Russia out of the global diesel export market. The UK's seizure of a shadow fleet tanker in the English Channel opens a new front in sanctions enforcement. Two conflicts — one in the Gulf, one in Eastern Europe — are simultaneously reshaping global energy flows in real time.
sources: Reuters, BBC, France 24, New York Times, Anadolu Agency, AP, CNBC, The Economist, Saudi Gazette, United24 Media, EADaily, The Star (Malaysia), Mathrubhumi English, Outlook India, Business Standard, Firstpost, Daily Sabah, Invezz, SCMP, NPR, The Guardian, Kyiv Independent, Meduza, Nairametrics, MSN, The Hill, Defense News, Al-Monitor, Asharq Al-Awsat
